text: The video shows a large aneurysmal hernia with a visible defect in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon makes an incision on the cephalad portion of the deep ring, using it as a marker.
The surgeon completes the abandonment of the sac and continues the dissection after developing a tissue flap, focusing on minimizing bleeding and trauma around the cord elements.
The video fast-forwards through the dissection process, focusing on the lateral dissection around the deep ring, where an intentional hole is visible.
The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum around the large defect of the aneurysmortal hernia, carefully manipulating the tissue to enhance exposure.
The surgeon begins medial dissection to facilitate dissection around the deep ring and complete the abandonment of the sac.
The surgeon grasps the peritoneum and carefully dissects away the cord structures and neurovascular elements, followed by an incision around the area of the deep ring.
The surgeon completes a circumferential incision around the deep ring, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon grabs the edge of the peritoneum to develop a clear view of the myopectenial orifice, focusing on the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon focuses on establishing a clear critical view of the surgical field to ensure there is an adequate pocket for mesh deployment.
The surgeon performs a precise dissection around the vas deferens, followed by parietalization of the surrounding vasculature.
The surgeon points out the external iliac vein, highlighting its location and orientation within the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to finalize the dissection but encounters difficulty visualizing the iliopubic tract due to the presence of a small cord lipoma.
The surgeon transects the small cord lipoma, revealing the iliopubic tract clearly. The dissection is finalized.
The surgical field is prepared for mesh placement and positioning, with a clear view established for the deployment of the mesh.
The surgeon places the mesh into the prepared pocket, securing it at Cooper's ligament and along the superior edge of the mesh.
The surgeon closes the flap from lateral to medial, completing the flap closure process.
